title: Genoa cake
subtitle: Light fruit cake with glacé cherries and almonds
excerpt: Genoa cake is a lighter, less dense alternative to Christmas cake — a fruit cake with glacé cherries, mixed peel, and flaked almonds pressed into the top. It needs no maturing time and is best within a week of baking.
sourceNotes: Genoa cake became a fixture of British domestic baking in the Victorian period, named for its supposed Italian origins though it is primarily a British form. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — Genoa cake appears in the cakes chapter as a lighter fruit cake alternative to the richer Christmas cake.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze wrapped. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 5–7 days in an airtight tin. No maturing required unlike richer fruit cakes.

glossaryTerms.definition: The step of rinsing and drying glacé cherries before adding them to a fruit cake batter. The sticky syrup coating causes cherries to sink to the bottom during baking; rinsing under warm water and thoroughly patting dry removes this coating so the cherries stay suspended in the crumb.

content.text: is not optional if you want the cherries distributed through the crumb rather than sitting in a layer at the bottom. Rinse under warm water, shake off, and dry thoroughly on kitchen paper before halving and adding to the batter.

Heat the oven to 160°C (140°C fan). Line a deep 20 cm round tin with baking paper. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time. Fold in {{self-raising-flour}}, then stir through {{sultanas}}, {{mixed-peel}}, {{glace-cherries}}, {{lemon}} zest, and {{whole-milk}}. Spoon into the tin and smooth the top. Scatter {{almonds-flaked}} over the surface and press them in gently. Bake for 70–75 minutes until a skewer comes out clean and the almonds are deep gold. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes before turning out.
